text: The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the fundus of the gallbladder, revealing the surrounding fatty tissue. The omentum is pushed down to improve visibility. The gallbladder comes into view, showing a stone stuck in its neck. The surgeon begins to remove the peritoneal cover from the sides of the gallbladder.

The surgeon removes the fat and peritoneal cover from the gallbladder, exposing the cystic duct and cystic artery. The cystic artery is clearly visible as it approaches the gallbladder, which connects to the biliary ducts, forming the common bile duct.

The surgeon mobilizes the gallbladder from the liver bed, achieving the Critical View of Safety by clearly exposing the cystic artery and duct. Anterior dissection is performed, removing fibro-fatty tissue and small vessels. The gallbladder fundus is grasped and pushed back to facilitate dissection, with posterior dissection also being carried out to separate the gallbladder from the liver.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, clearly visualizing the cystic duct, cystic artery, and liver bed. The posterior branch of the cystic artery and the main cystic artery are identified. Clips are applied to the cystic duct and arteries to prevent bleeding and bile leakage during gallbladder removal.

The surgeon applies two clips to the cystic artery, securing both the anterior and posterior branches together. Careful attention is given to applying a clip on the cystic duct without causing tenting or pulling on the common hepatic duct and common bile duct. The clip is pressed to ensure fluid is expelled from the tissue, preventing slippage.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut the cystic duct, which is now free, while small vessels, likely lymphatics, are visible. The posterior branch of the cystic artery is clearly seen going to the gallbladder. The gallbladder is being removed from its fossa using a cautery hook, with the peritoneum and fascia covering being dissected away. Small veins and arteries are managed to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon clips down vessels connected to the liver and uses coagulation to address bleeding points. The gallbladder is extracted from its fossa, and suction is applied to remove a small amount of blood. A broad spatula is used to apply coagulation to a wider area, effectively stopping the bleeding.
